key_insight: The paper shows that weakly-coupled MDPs and restless bandits can be learned with polynomial sample and computational complexity by exploiting structure, rather than treating them as huge tabular MDPs.
engineering_relevance: This enables scalable planning and learning for large multi-agent/resource-allocation systems where naive state-space expansion would be computationally infeasible.
method: Use a plug-in pipeline: estimate an empirical model from generative samples, then run an efficient planning algorithm.
Replace bias-function-based analysis with a Lyapunov function and drift-transfer argument to compare true vs. empirical dynamics.
Derive perturbation bounds for the LP relaxation to control policy quality under model estimation error.
evidence_signal: The abstract claims finite-sample PAC guarantees under a generative model, with polynomial scaling in the number of arms and an O(1/√N) optimality gap for fully heterogeneous WCMDPs.
watch_item: The guarantees depend on structural assumptions and a generative sampling model, so real-world deployment may face mismatch, estimation cost, or weaker performance under model misspecification.
paper_title: Lyapunov-Based Sample Complexity Analysis for Weakly-Coupled MDPs
